_description: Functional programming anti-patterns for TypeScript/React — inspired by the Open Souls immutable WorkingMemory paradigm: const-by-default, pure functions, declarative transformations, no mutation. Additional rules from Matt Pocock, Christian Rackerseder, and Marek Honzal.

rules.message: [architecture] Class component — use functional components with hooks. Classes mix concerns and break composability
[architecture] Mutating array method (.push/.pop/.shift/.unshift/.splice) — use spread, concat, filter, or slice to return new arrays
[architecture] delete operator mutates objects — use destructuring rest or Object.fromEntries(Object.entries().filter()) to create new objects
[architecture] Object.assign — use spread { ...target, ...source } for immutability. Object.assign mutates its first argument unless it is {}
[convention] var declaration — use const (preferred) or let. var has function scope and hoisting bugs
[architecture] Class-based state/props access — use useState/useReducer hooks in functional components
[testability] Math.random() is non-deterministic — inject as parameter or use a seeded PRNG for testability
[debugging-residue] console.* call — use a logger abstraction or remove before committing
[architecture] Type assertion to any — defeats TypeScript's type safety. Use unknown, generics, or proper type narrowing
[async-misuse] new Promise() constructor — prefer async/await or composing existing promises. Raw constructors often indicate callback-wrapping that async/await handles better

[architecture] TypeScript enum — use 'as const' objects or string literal unions instead. Enums emit runtime code, have inconsistent behavior, and break with isolatedModules/bundlers (const enum is worse).
rules._source: Matt Pocock (totaltypescript.com), Christian Rackerseder (echooff.dev), Marek Honzal (marekhonzal.com)

rules.message: [architecture] TypeScript namespace — use ES modules instead. Namespaces are a legacy pattern that adds non-erasable runtime code.
